topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt like sandpaper under my fingertips as I unfolded the document. Every carefully chosen adjective, every neatly summarized achievement, was a calculated falsehood. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake of air that did nothing to settle the churning in my stomach. I stared at the dates, the career milestones—all belonging to someone else, meticulously crafted to fit a narrative I never authorized. A slow, cold wave washed over me, leaving my limbs heavy and unresponsive. I crumpled the paper, the sound a brittle, angry rustle in the quiet room. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping back with a grating screech that echoed the sudden, violent shift inside me. "This is a lie," I managed, the words tasting like ash. I didn't wait for a reply; I just walked toward the door, needing distance from the suffocating arrangement of fabricated history.

[story 2]

I traced the elegant script with a shaking finger, my knuckles turning white against the parchment. The details—the pivotal moments, the defining struggles—were all alien, imposed upon my own life like a foreign skin. A deep, burning sensation rose in my chest, tightening my throat until speaking felt impossible. I reread a passage about my early career, and a bitter laugh escaped my lips, devoid of any humor. It was a sound wrenched from deep within, raw and ugly. I slammed the file shut, the impact jarring my whole frame. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles vibrating beneath my skin. I paced the length of the study, my footsteps heavy and deliberate, each stride a declaration of utter rejection. I needed to dismantle this fabrication, piece by agonizing piece.

[story 3]

The realization hit me not as a sudden shock, but as a slow, creeping dread that solidified into pure, concentrated fury. I sat at the antique desk, the glow of the lamp illuminating the damning text. My vision tunneled slightly. I felt a distinct heat radiating from my core, an internal pressure building until I had to press my palms flat onto the wood to keep from shaking uncontrollably. Every line of text was an insult, a deliberate erasure of my reality. I snatched a nearby pen, not to write, but to grip it until my fingers ached. With a sudden, sharp movement, I swept the entire stack off the desk, sending the papers scattering across the floor like fallen debris. I stood over the mess, breathing deeply, trying to force the frantic energy down, though the tremor in my hands betrayed the sheer force of my indignation.
